question: Classify this flop: K♥ 7♦ 2♣
draws: None — rainbow, unconnected, no flush or straight draws possible.
explanation: Rainbow (three suits), unconnected, high-card board. No flush draws, no open-ended straight draws. Hands are either made or not — opponents can't profitably chase. Value bet strong hands without protection worries.

question: Classify this flop: Q♠ 7♠ 2♠
draws: Flush draws — any single spade has a draw, made flushes possible.
explanation: Monotone board — all three cards the same suit. Anyone with a single spade has a flush draw, and flopped flushes are possible. Extremely wet despite being unconnected.

question: Classify this flop: 9♠ 8♠ 7♦
draws: Flush draw (spades), multiple straight draws (JT, T6, 65, J6), made straights possible (JT, 65).
explanation: Connected AND two-tone. JT and 65 already made straights. Any T, J, 6, or 5 has straight draw equity, plus spade flush draws. One of the wettest flop types.

question: Classify this flop: Q♦ T♦ 8♥
draws: Flush draw (diamonds), straight draws (J9 made, K9, 97, AJ gutshots).
explanation: Connected and two-tone. J9 flopped a straight. KJ, 97, J7 have open-enders or gutshots. Diamond draws add more. Classic wet board.

question: Classify this flop: K♣ 8♦ 2♣
draws: Club flush draw only — no realistic straight draws.
explanation: Two-tone (two clubs) brings a flush draw, but unconnected ranks mean no straight draws. Drier than connected boards, wetter than rainbow. Hardin calls these semi-wet.

question: Classify this flop: A♥ K♦ 4♣
draws: Only gutshots (QJ, QT need runner or gutshot straight).
explanation: Rainbow, mostly unconnected. QJ has a gutshot to Broadway but that's the only draw. High-card heavy — connects hard with raising ranges. Effectively dry.

question: Classify this flop: 7♥ 7♦ 2♣
explanation: Paired rainbow board. No flush draws, no straight draws. Driest board type in poker. Either someone has trips/a pair or nothing.

question: Classify this flop: J♥ T♥ 9♥
draws: Made flushes, made straights (KQ, Q8, 87), every heart a flush draw, all straight draws live.
explanation: Monotone AND fully connected. The wettest possible flop type. KQ/87 flopped straights, any heart flops a flush draw, sets are vulnerable to everything.

question: Classify this flop: A♠ 9♦ 4♠
draws: Spade flush draw; wheel gutshots (23, 35, 25 need two cards — minimal).
explanation: Two-tone brings the flush draw. Ranks are disconnected so straight draws are negligible. Semi-wet: protect strong hands against the flush draw but otherwise value-focused.

question: Classify this flop: 6♣ 5♦ 4♣
draws: Made straights (87, 32), open-enders everywhere (any 7, 3), club flush draw.
explanation: Low connected two-tone board. 87 and 32 made straights, any 7 or 3 has huge equity, clubs add flush draws. Low wet boards smash calling ranges (suited connectors) harder than raising ranges.

question: Classify this flop: K♦ Q♣ 2♥
draws: Straight draws only: JT open-ender, AJ/AT/J9 gutshots. No flush draws.
explanation: Rainbow but two broadways are connected. JT has an open-ender, AJ a gutshot. No flush draws. The connectivity makes it semi-wet despite the rainbow suits.

question: Classify this flop: T♦ 9♣ 2♦
draws: Diamond flush draw, JQ/J8/87 straight draws around the T9.
explanation: Two-tone with two connected middle cards. QJ, J8, 87 have straight draws, diamonds have flush draws. The deuce is a blank. Semi-wet leaning wet.

question: Classify this flop: A♣ A♦ 7♠
explanation: Paired ace rainbow board. Whoever has the ace has trips; no draws exist. Extremely dry. Bluffing is effective because the board hits so few hands.

question: Classify this flop: 8♥ 4♦ 3♥
draws: Heart flush draw; 65/52/A2/A5 wheel-ish gutshots and open-enders.
explanation: Two-tone low board. 65 has an open-ender, 52/62 gutshots, hearts draw to flush. Low boards favor the preflop caller's range (suited connectors, small pairs).

question: Classify this flop: Q♥ J♦ T♣
draws: Made straights (AK, 98, K9), every broadway has a draw, no flush draws (rainbow).
explanation: Three connected broadways, rainbow. AK and 98 flopped straights; K9 too. Every AQ/KQ/KJ/AJ/T9 has pairs+draws. Rainbow saves it from being maximum wet, but it's still very wet.

question: Board: Q♦ T♦ 8♥. Which made straight is possible on this flop?
explanation: J9 makes the nut straight (J-9 fills Q-T-8 → Q-J-T-9-8). KJ and 97 are draws (gutshot/open-ender), not made hands. This distinction — made vs drawing — drives every postflop decision.

question: Board: 9♠ 8♠ 7♦. Which TWO hands flopped made straights?
explanation: JT makes J-T-9-8-7, 65 makes 9-8-7-6-5. T9/76 made pairs+draws. On triple-connected boards always check which two-card combos complete straights immediately.

question: Board: K♣ 8♦ 2♣. A player holds A♣ 5♣. What do they have?
explanation: Two clubs in hand + two on board = four clubs, needing one more: nut flush draw (ace-high). ~36% to complete by river with two cards to come.

question: Board: J♥ T♥ 9♥. A player holds Q♥ 2♦. What do they have?
explanation: Q♥ gives a flush draw (four hearts). The Q also gives an open-ended straight draw (K or 8 completes). Monster combo draw — roughly 54% equity vs one pair.

question: Board: A♥ K♦ 4♣. A player holds Q♠ J♠. What do they have?
explanation: QJ on AK4 needs exactly a ten for Broadway (A-K-Q-J-T): gutshot, 4 outs, ~16% by river. Not an open-ender — only one rank completes it.

question: Board: 6♣ 5♦ 4♣. A player holds 8♣ 7♣. What do they have?
explanation: 87 completes 8-7-6-5-4 — a made straight — and two clubs add a flush draw to improve to even stronger. Monster hand: fast-play it.

question: Flop: K♥ 7♦ 2♣. Turn: J♦. How does the J♦ turn change this dry flop?
options: Brings backdoor flush draw + gutshots — now semi-wet
explanation: Hardin's exact example: the dry K72 rainbow turns semi-wet on the J♦. AT/QT now have gutshots, diamond hands picked up backdoor flush draws. Hands that floated the flop just gained equity — barreling now needs more caution.

question: Flop: Q♦ T♦ 8♥. Turn: 4♥. How does the 4♥ turn change this wet flop?
options: Adds a backdoor heart draw — board stays wet, slightly wetter
explanation: The 4♥ completes nothing but adds backdoor heart possibilities. K♥J♥-type hands gained extra equity. All original draws (diamonds, straights) remain live. Wet stays wet.

question: Flop: 9♠ 8♠ 7♦. Turn: 6♠. How does the 6♠ turn change this board?
options: Completes flushes AND the T-or-5 straight — maximum danger
explanation: Disaster card for one-pair hands: the 6♠ completes every spade flush draw AND now any T or 5 plays a straight. Two-pair and sets are in serious trouble. Value hands must re-evaluate immediately.

question: Flop: K♣ 8♦ 2♣. Turn: 8♥. How does pairing the board (8♥) change things?
options: Trips possible, flush draw still live — counterfeits some two-pair outs
explanation: Pairing the 8 means 8x has trips. The club draw is still live. Paired boards reduce two-pair combos and make bluffing harder against stations who call with any 8.

question: Flop: A♥ K♦ 4♣. Turn: T♠. How does the T♠ turn change this board?
options: QJ completes Broadway — the only straight is now made
explanation: The T completes QJ's gutshot — Broadway (A-K-Q-J-T) is now made. If villain floated the flop with QJ, this is their card. Also gives J-x new gutshots. High boards + connecting turns favor preflop raisers' ranges.

question: Flop: 7♥ 7♦ 2♣. Turn: 2♦. How does the 2♦ turn change this paired board?
options: Double-paired — full houses possible, flush draw arrives
explanation: Board is now double-paired (77-22): any 7 has a full house, any 2 has a smaller one. The 2♦ also starts a backdoor diamond draw. Overpairs now beat fewer combos — counterfeiting matters.

question: Flop: 6♣ 5♦ 4♣. Turn: 3♣. How does the 3♣ turn change this board?
options: Completes flushes AND 7/2 straights — extremely dangerous
explanation: Triple threat: the 3♣ completes club flushes, any 7 now plays 7-6-5-4-3, any 2 plays 6-5-4-3-2. One of the worst possible turns for a flop value hand like a set of sixes.

question: Flop: Q♥ J♦ T♣. Turn: T♠. How does pairing the ten change this wet board?
options: Trips/boats arrive — straights lose value, bluff-catching changes
explanation: Pairing the T means flopped straights (AK, 98) are no longer the nuts — TT/QT/JT boats and trips beat them. Straight holders must slow down. Paired wet boards shift power to sets and two pair.

question: Flop: A♠ 9♦ 4♠. Turn: K♠. How does the K♠ turn change this board?
options: Completes the spade flush — flush is now made
explanation: Third spade completes every flush draw. AK-type hands also picked up top two. Any single high spade now has a strong made hand. Bet-sizing and calling ranges change completely once the flush arrives.

question: Flop: 8♥ 4♦ 3♥. Turn: 2♥. How does the 2♥ turn change this board?
options: Flush completes AND A5/65 straights arrive
explanation: The 2♥ completes heart flushes AND now A5 plays the wheel (5-4-3-2-A) while 65 makes 6-5-4-3-2. Low coordinated runouts hit calling ranges hard — proceed carefully with overpairs.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Ace-high dry boards massively favor the preflop raiser. The CO has all the strong aces — AK flopped top two, plus AQ, AJ, AA. The BB rarely holds an ace (most good Ax 3-bets or folds pre). The caller can't continue enough; bet small and relentlessly.

scenario: Villain (UTG, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: KQ7 rainbow favors the tight UTG raiser. They hold KQ (top two), AK, KJ, AQ, plus overpairs. The BB's wide range connects weakly — a few Kx and Qx but mostly air. Strong c-bet board.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Even with a flush draw present, AQ5 favors the raiser. The CO has AQ (top two), AA, AK, plus spade draws to the nuts. The BB has few aces or queens. The two-tone texture means bet for value AND protection, but the range edge is clearly the raiser's.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Middling connected boards favor the wide caller. The BB has 87s (top two), 65s, T9s, 55, 66, plus tons of draws. The CO's overpairs are strong but face a board smashing the caller's suited connectors. The caller's nutted+draw share is higher. Check-heavy for the raiser.

scenario: Villain (MP, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: 987 rainbow favors the caller despite the raiser's overpairs. The BB holds T9, 98, 87, 76, JT, plus 65 and 56 — made straights and huge draws. MP's AA/KK are strong but vulnerable. The caller connects far better with this texture.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: T96 two-tone strongly favors the caller. The BB has T9 (top two), 98, 87 draws, plus all the diamond flush draws and 66/99 sets. The caller's combined made+draw equity beats the raiser's overpair-heavy range. Caller can check-raise aggressively.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Low connected boards favor the caller despite the raiser having more overpairs. On 654 those overpairs are vulnerable to every straight and two-pair the caller holds. The BB has 87s, 76s, 65s, 55, 44 — straights, sets, draws. Their nutted share is higher even though overpairs look strong.

scenario: Villain (BTN, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: 542 two-tone favors the caller. The BB defends 54s (top two), 53s, 43s, A5s, A4s, plus spade draws and wheel gutshots. The BTN's wide range has overcards and overpairs that whiffed. The caller's draw-heavy connection wins this texture.

scenario: Villain (UTG, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Paired king boards favor the tight raiser. UTG has more Kx (AK, KQ, KJ) giving trips, plus overpairs. The BB rarely has a king in their defending range. Paired boards reduce the caller's two-pair combos and let the raiser barrel relentlessly — bluffs work because villain so often has nothing.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Paired 8s on a low board narrowly favors the raiser. Their overpairs (99-AA) all beat an unpaired 8, and they have the same trip-8 combos (88 is in both ranges). The CO's strong+ share edges out the BB's. It's close — the key lesson is that paired boards generally favor the preflop aggressor because the caller's two-pair combos are removed. Barrel with overpairs, but size down.

scenario: Villain (UTG, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: K72 rainbow is the textbook raiser board. UTG's tight range is full of Kx and overpairs that smashed it; the BB's wide range has few kings and connects poorly with disconnected high cards. C-bet almost the entire range — the caller folds far too often to make calling profitable.

scenario: Villain (MP, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Q83 rainbow favors the raiser. MP holds QJ, QT, AQ, KQ (top pair good kicker) plus overpairs. The BB's Qx is weaker (Q9, QT) and most of their range is air. Standard small c-bet board.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. Two players call. Flop comes. (multiway)
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: On JT9 multiway the callers' ranges are favored — 25% nutted vs 16% — and nutted hands matter far more with two opponents. Someone often has a straight (KQ, Q8, 87) or a monster draw. The raiser's overpairs crater in value multiway. Lesson: only continue with nutted hands or strong draws; check everything marginal and never bluff into two players on a board this wet.

scenario: Villain (UTG, TAG) opens. Hero (BB) calls. Flop comes.
question: Whose range does this flop favor?
explanation: Paired aces overwhelmingly favor the raiser. UTG has many more aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) giving trips, plus the BB almost never has an ace. This is the driest board type — bluff relentlessly since villain connects almost never, and value bet trips thin.
